transcription: Hey, welcome to Models and Agents for Beginners episode two from March 8, 2026. Let's break down today's coolest AI news so anyone can understand it. Let's go. Claude scanned this massive pile of code. Think of code as the invisible instructions that make a browser work, like a recipe book for your computer. And it found over 100 bugs. Let me build on that analogy. Picture Claude as an eagle-eyed proofreader going over enormous book. That's because AI like Claude learns from billions of examples of code, getting really good at spotting inconsistencies that could lead to problems, like a spot where a hacker might slip in. Today, let's talk about something that trips up a lot of people new to AI, hallucinations, and large language models. These are those moments when an AI like ChatGPT makes up facts that sound totally real, but aren't true at all. Like claiming a unicorn won the Olympics when you ask about sports history. Okay, this one's a bit tricky, but stick with me. We'll build it up step-by-step. Imagine you're playing a game of telephone with a huge chain of friends. You whisper a story to the first person's, they pass it on. But by the end, it's all twisted with extra details that weren't in the original, because each friend guesses a little to fill in what they didn't hear clearly. That's a lot like how a language model operates. These models are basically massive prediction machines, trained on billions of words from books, websites, and online chats. They learn patterns, like knowing that after the sky is, it often says blue based on what they've seen a ton of times. Step one, when you ask a question, the AI breaks it down into tiny chunks called tokens. Think of tokens as puzzle pieces, roughly one per word or part of a word, like breaking hello into hell and low sometimes to make patterns easier to handle. Step two, the AI calculates probabilities for what comes next, kind of like rolling dice that are weighted toward common patterns. It's predicting the most likely continuation. But if the training data had weird, incomplete, or conflicting info, the math inside can get messy. Researchers call this spilled energy. Imagine you're pouring water into a bunch of cups, but some overflows because the cups aren't big enough, leading to invented details to fill the gaps. Step three, this spilled energy leaves traces in the AI's internal calculations, like crumbs from a cookie you snuck in weren't supposed to eat. A new method spots these traces without needing to retrain the whole model, which is huge because retraining is like rebuilding a house from scratch. Step four, by measuring that spilled energy, the system can flag when the AI is likely hallucinating, helping make responses more trustworthy. And that's basically how hallucinations happen in language models. They're awesome at spotting patterns, but can be overconfident guesses, and this trick helps catch them to keep things honest, whether you're using AI for homework help, or sparking ideas for creative writing. Not so scary once we break it down, right? It shows AI isn't magic. It's just really good at patterns, with some clever fixes for when it slips up. Think of parameters as the building blocks or brain cells in its digital mind, giving it smarts without needing a super powerful computer to run. What makes it special? It's a multimodal model, which means it can handle different types of input all at once, like text and images. It's like turning your phone into a smart helper. Go play around and see what you discover. OK, quick bits to wrap up the news. These are fast and fun.
